_provenance.note: Web-research → adversarial-verify → rubric-score; cited + confidence-scored. Enriched 2026-06-20 with deeper magazine, builder, dealer and brokerage sourcing.

facts.value: EC Category A (Ocean), the highest recreational-craft category, certifying the design for wind above force 8 and significant wave heights above 4m. Amel has built ocean-category boats for decades and the 60 is its world-cruising flagship.

sources.title: Amel 60 (Amel) - Sailboat specifications - Boat-Specs.com
Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: Lead/cast-iron ballast keel of about 7.2 tonnes on a deep GRP keel stub. Light displacement is roughly 26,000 kg (Yachting World) and loaded displacement about 27.9 tonnes (YACHT.de test), giving a ballast ratio of approximately 26 percent. Single fixed keel, draft about 2.35m.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - AMEL
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT
Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: The Amel 60 is the first Amel to abandon the brand's signature ketch rig in favour of a single-masted sloop with a tall carbon mast. Amel decided the ketch reduced the volume of the central cockpit, did not suit the wide modern aft sections, and that two masts were extra work; modern electric furling makes a taller single rig easy to handle short-handed. It is the modern successor to the ketch-rigged Amel 55.

sources.title: In all Discretion, Amel has Stopped the construction of Ketches, in Favor of Sloops - Yachting Art Magazine
Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: Named European Yacht of the Year 2020 in the Luxury Cruiser category by the panel of leading European sailing magazines (announced January 2020). Widely described as a benchmark for build quality in its class.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: Upwind sail area is roughly 165-170 m² (mainsail about 77-78 m², 110 percent genoa about 88-92 m²). Yachting World cites a sail-area-to-displacement ratio of 19.7, which sits in the reasonable-performance band; the boat sails to around 7 knots in 8-10 knots of wind and reaches close to its ~10-knot hull speed under a gennaker, with reported 200-plus-mile days. Upwind pointing is only average at about 50 degrees.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT
Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: Diesel hydronic heating is an optional upgrade priced at about €18,000; it is not part of the base specification. The boat is built primarily for warm-water world cruising rather than high-latitude work, so heating is a buyer-added system.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: Solid GRP laminate below the waterline with a PVC foam-core sandwich above the waterline, resin-infused, and high-density insulation throughout the boat (its stated purpose is noise reduction rather than a cold-climate thermal barrier). The engine room is its own insulated, sound-deadened compartment.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: Keel-stepped carbon-fibre furling mast supplied by Lorima, fitted as standard. Only a small-diameter compression post passes through the saloon, so the cabin stays open and light.

sources.title: Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT
Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: Six watertight compartments are part of the standard build, with insulated compartments and watertight bulkheads called out by the builder. This subdivision is a core Amel safety feature and a major reason the boat is described as exceptionally hard to sink.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - AMEL
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: Forward helm on the port side of Amel's signature deep central cockpit, fully sheltered by a hardtop and wrap-around windshield with a sliding sunroof. The skipper can run the electric furling for main, genoa and staysail from inside the enclosure without going on deck, giving near-pilothouse weather protection.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: Numerous opening deck hatches and opening portholes provide natural ventilation, and the central cockpit hardtop has an opening sunroof and sliding roof sections to vent the protected cockpit. The deep saloon windows and distributed portholes keep air moving below.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - AMEL
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: The fixed hardtop over the central cockpit gives full standing headroom and large safety-glass windows, providing genuine permanent shade rather than a fabric bimini. Roof sections open electrically, and a fabric enclosure is available to fully close the cockpit.

sources.title: Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: A 180-litre refrigerator is standard. The 720Ah lithium house bank also powers an electric/induction galley and electric oven, so refrigeration and a freezer can run on stored power. Additional freezer and icemaker options are available but specific extra-freezer volumes are not independently confirmed.

sources.title: Amel 60 (Amel) - Sailboat specifications - Boat-Specs.com
AMEL 60 - BUILD TO ORDER | Naos Yachts

facts.value: A watermaker is an optional upgrade priced at about €18,500; it is not standard. The specific output rate is not confirmed in accessible sources, so it should be treated as an option to budget for rather than a known-capacity fitting.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: The deep central cockpit is sheltered from wind and sun, and the wide saloon with distributed opening portholes and hatches encourages cross-flow below. Air conditioning is an optional upgrade (about €26,000) for full climate control in the tropics.

sources.title: Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com
Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: A Furuno hydraulic autopilot is standard equipment, controlled from the main helm console and reported to steer extremely precisely and reliably during testing. Electronic autopilot is the primary self-steering method on this boat.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: No mechanical windvane self-steering option is documented; the boat is engineered around its electric autopilot. Owners wanting wind-driven steering redundancy would need to fit an aftermarket vane, which is not a factory option.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: Standard power package is a 17.5 kVA diesel generator, a 720Ah lithium house bank, and a roughly 530W solar array on the hardtop. The 900-litre fuel tank gives an estimated 130 hours of motoring range. The boat is electrically rich, but the lithium bank plus heavy AC loads (induction galley, watermaker, air conditioning) mean the generator is run regularly rather than the boat being engineless-autonomous for long periods.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - BUILD TO ORDER | Naos Yachts
Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: Large hydraulic bathing platform with an integrated ladder, and a wide, clear aft deck (freed up by dropping the mizzen) that doubles as a sunbathing and dinghy-stowage area.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - AMEL
Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: High freeboard, with large rectangular deadlights set into the topsides to break up and visually reduce the apparent height of the hull. Specific freeboard measurements are not published.

sources.title: AMEL 60 - AMEL

facts.value: Clean, clutter-free side decks ringed by a solid stainless-steel guardrail that encircles the whole deck and stands higher than conventional guard wires. Reviewers rate it among the easiest and most secure decks to move around on in its size and style.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Bluewater Icon: Amel 60 – no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: The encircling solid guardrail and high bulwark make it hard to slip over the side, and the central cockpit sits near the hull's pivot point where motion is least, which helps crew stay aboard and stay functional. No specific factory man-overboard recovery system is documented.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: An optional self-tacking cutter rig with an electric furling staysail is available for about €20,000, and the test boat carried it. With the staysail set the boat sails well and stays easy to manage; combined with the sheltered cockpit and push-button furling, it gives a sound short-handed heavy-weather setup.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: No magazine storm test or documented Amel 60 survival-conditions report was found. The boat is CE Category A certified and its design strengths for heavy weather are well documented: a deep sheltered cockpit at the hull's pivot point, six watertight compartments, an encircling solid guardrail, and all-electric furling controllable from inside the enclosure with manual backups. One owner couple who took delivery and sailed it down to the Canaries for an ARC described the maintenance and admin learning curve as heavy at first but gained full confidence in the boat with coastal hops before the crossing.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: Launch base price was about €1,650,000 (ex VAT) in 2019-2020; by mid-2024 the German test quoted €2,499,000 including 19 percent VAT for a well-equipped boat. Brokerage asking prices run roughly €1,550,000 to €2,500,000 depending on year and equipment, with the oldest hulls dating to 2020. Because these are near-new boats, the real money is in optioning/commissioning rather than age-driven refit.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT
Amel 60 for Sale | Used Amel 60 Prices | TWW Yachts

facts.value: Standard guidance for stainless wire standing rigging is replacement at roughly 10-15 years, sooner with hard offshore use. The Amel 60's first hulls date to 2020, so at six-plus years they are well short of a full re-rig; the realistic near-term task is inspection and end-fitting checks rather than replacement.

sources.title: Cost for standing rigging replacement

facts.value: The 60 uses a maintenance-free teak-effect synthetic deck rather than real teak, removing the recurring teak-deck restoration cost that burdens older bluewater boats. This meaningfully lowers long-term deck upkeep versus traditional teak-decked cruisers.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

facts.value: The main documented concerns are a furling-genoa halyard-wrap risk (if the halyard is stowed slack it can wrap the furling swivel and load the forestay, a failure mode seen on the Amel 50 predecessor), weak steering feel from long cable runs to the twin rudders, an absence of propwalk that makes tight powering hard without a stern thruster, and grab handles in the guest cockpit described as angular and sharp. Electric sheet winches positioned behind the helmsman also raise an accidental-activation safety note. These are handling/ergonomic cautions rather than structural defects.

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality
Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect | YACHT

facts.value: Strong factory support through the official Amel network, with three service bases covering the main cruising routes: La Rochelle (Atlantic/headquarters), Hyeres (Mediterranean), and Le Marin in Martinique (Caribbean). Multi-skilled teams and a comprehensive standard specification keep the boat well supported, though many systems are proprietary Amel installations best serviced through that network.

sources.title: AMEL… service and support par excellence - AMEL
AMEL 60 - AMEL

praise: Named 2020 European Yacht of the Year in the Luxury Cruiser category
Engine access via gas-strut cockpit floor is described as the envy of the bluewater cruising world
Central cockpit sits at the hull's pivot point, giving the least motion in rough conditions
Comprehensive standard specification removes most outfitting guesswork for buyers
Well built and reassuringly easy to handle short-handed
Among the easiest and most secure decks to move around on for its size and style
All-electric furling for main, genoa and staysail controllable from inside the sheltered cockpit
Solid laminate hull below the waterline with foam-sandwich topsides, resin-infused
Light-filled, modern interior styling and a genuinely usable forward navigation station
Household-grade galley with induction cooking and electric oven run off the 720Ah lithium bank
Six watertight compartments make the boat extremely hard to sink
Furuno autopilot steers extremely precisely and reliably
Maintenance-free teak-effect decks avoid the recurring cost of real teak
Strong factory support with service bases on the Atlantic, Mediterranean and Caribbean cruising routes
complaints: Light, indirect steering feel from long cable runs to the twin rudders
Twin rudders give no propwalk, so tight powering is hard without an optional stern/bow thruster
Grab handles in the guest cockpit are stylish but angular and sharp
Electric sheet winches sited behind the helmsman raise an accidental-activation concern
Only one standard interior layout is offered
Performance needs real wind to move the roughly 26-28 tonne displacement
Forward helm position is unconventional and divides opinion
Delivery lead times have run beyond two years on sustained demand
Heavy systems load means the generator gets run regularly to keep the lithium bank and AC loads fed
known_issues: Furling-genoa halyard wrap: a slack or poorly stowed halyard can wrap the furling swivel and load the forestay (a failure mode seen on the Amel 50 predecessor)
Weak steering feedback from long cable runs to the twin rudders
No propwalk with twin rudders limits close-quarters maneuvering without a thruster
Sharp guest-cockpit grab handles are uncomfortable in prolonged use
Electric winches behind the helm can be activated without a clear view of the load

sources.title: Cost for standing rigging replacement

refit_items.action: Add AIS transponder, second chartplotter or tablet nav, radar if absent, Starlink/Iridium comms, redundant handheld GPS and VHF, autopilot service

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.action: Add solar capacity beyond the standard ~530W hardtop array and expand or future-proof the 720Ah lithium bank to reduce generator hours

sources.title: AMEL 60 - BUILD TO ORDER | Naos Yachts

refit_items.action: Install the factory or equivalent watermaker so the boat is provisioning-independent on passage

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.system: Air conditioning (if not fitted at build)
refit_items.action: Install reverse-cycle air conditioning for tropical liveaboard comfort; factory option around €26,000

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.action: Add or replace electric furling Code 0 (~€18,000) and/or gennaker (~€12,500) and a self-tacking cutter staysail (~€20,000) for short-handed passage making

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.action: Fit a retractable stern thruster to compensate for the twin rudders' lack of propwalk in tight quarters

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.system: Diesel hydronic heating (if cruising high latitudes)
refit_items.action: Install the factory diesel hydronic heating option; relevant only for buyers heading to colder waters

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

refit_items.action: Offshore liferaft, EPIRB, jacklines and harnesses, storm sails, plus heavier anchor/chain and windlass service for world cruising

sources.title: Amel 60 review: This modern cruiser is a true benchmark for quality

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furling-genoa halyard pre-rigged plus a halyard organiser or anti-wrap collar
provisioning_specific.why: Halyard wrap around the furling swivel is the best-documented critical failure mode for this design (and the Amel 50 before it). Carrying a pre-rigged spare and keeping the halyard tensioned prevents a forestay-threatening wrap on passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Emergency tiller and steering-cable spares for the twin-rudder system
provisioning_specific.why: Reviewers flag the long steering cable runs to the twin rudders and the light, indirect feel. Cable runs are a known wear point, so a confirmed emergency tiller fit and spare cable/quadrant parts are sensible before a long passage.

provisioning_specific.item: 17.5 kVA generator service kit (filters, impellers, oil, belts)
provisioning_specific.why: The standard 17.5 kVA diesel generator is run regularly to feed the lithium bank, induction galley, watermaker and air conditioning. In remote cruising grounds a full service kit keeps the boat's electrical heart running between Amel service bases.

provisioning_specific.item: Lithium BMS fuses, shunts and a cell-monitoring tool
provisioning_specific.why: The 720Ah lithium house bank powers cooking, refrigeration and house loads. BMS protection spares and a monitoring tool let you diagnose a cell-level issue before it takes down the whole bank, since these proprietary parts are hard to source underway.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filters and membrane pickling solution (if watermaker fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: The optional watermaker is a major passage-readiness system. Tropical waters foul membranes quickly, so pre-filter cartridges and pickling chemicals keep it healthy across long gaps between use.

provisioning_specific.item: Carbon mast and Lorima furling-mast inspection and service spares
provisioning_specific.why: The keel-stepped Lorima carbon furling mast is central to the boat and proprietary. Furling-mast bearing service parts and a delamination/inspection routine protect the single biggest rig component.

provisioning_specific.item: Watertight-door and hatch gasket spares for the six compartments
provisioning_specific.why: The six-compartment subdivision is a core safety feature, but it only works if the seals do. Gaskets degrade over years, so spares keep the watertight boundaries intact for bluewater work.

provisioning_specific.item: Padded or sleeved covers for the guest-cockpit grab handles
provisioning_specific.why: The guest-cockpit grab handles are described as angular and sharp. Chafe-guard sleeves reduce hand injury when crew grip them under load in a seaway, at trivial cost.

provisioning_specific.item: Provisioning plan for a couple: 700L water plus watermaker, large fridge and freezer, induction galley
provisioning_specific.why: With 700L freshwater, a 180L fridge, and an electric/induction galley fed by lithium, a cruising couple can stock for multi-week passages; the watermaker (if fitted) makes water effectively unlimited, so the binding constraint is freezer space and dry-stores rather than tankage.

provisioning_specific.item: Stern thruster (if not fitted) for marina and anchorage handling
provisioning_specific.why: Twin rudders give no propwalk, so the boat is awkward in tight quarters under power. A stern thruster materially improves docking and anchorage safety on a 60-foot, ~27-tonne boat handled by two.

gaps: stability.avs - Angle of Vanishing Stability not published by Amel or in any accessible source
stability.stix - STIX numerical value not published in accessible sources
self_steering.electrical_autonomy - exact engineless battery autonomy hours not published; depends on load and solar yield
tropical.watermaker - optional watermaker output rate not confirmed in accessible sources
ergonomics.freeboard - specific freeboard measurement not published
heavy_weather.storm_report - no magazine storm test or documented Amel 60 survival-conditions report located; only design-feature and delivery-passage evidence exists

removed.reason: No source supports a '50-liter boiler capacity'. The €18,000 heating cost is confirmed; the boiler-volume sub-detail appears fabricated and is dropped.
Factually wrong. YACHT.de states the mast is a keel-stepped carbon Lorima furling mast. The earlier 'deck-stepped' wording was a direct error and is corrected.
The specific 240 L/h output appears in no accessible source. The €18,500 cost is confirmed; the production rate is unverified and removed.
Only the 180L refrigerator is confirmed. The added '80L freezer plus saloon freezer' detail is not independently confirmed and is removed as a hard fact.
Earlier records stored fixed USD conversions of EUR option prices (heating, watermaker, AC). Exchange rates move, so option costs are described in EUR with indicative USD bands only.

removed.key: refit.known_issues — 'at least five Amel 50s with damaged forestays'
removed.reason: The specific count comes from the AmelYachtOwners groups.io forum, which is paywalled (HTTP 402) and cannot be independently confirmed. Kept only as an unverified note.
The builder confirms an aft platform with integrated ladder; the 'hydraulic' descriptor is on the builder page but was previously cited to an inaccessible third-party listing. Re-sourced to amel.fr.
Displacement figures vary by source (26,000 kg light to 27,900 kg loaded), so any single ballast-ratio percentage is approximate. Stated as roughly 26 percent rather than an exact figure.

fabrication_flags: cold.heating: a '50-liter boiler capacity' sub-detail appears in no source and looks like invented precision; removed.
tropical.watermaker: a '240 liters/hour' output appears in no accessible source; the €18,500 cost is confirmed but the rate is unverified.
cold.keel_stepped_mast: an earlier 'deck-stepped' claim was a factual inversion; the mast is keel-stepped carbon (Lorima) per YACHT.de and has been corrected.
refit.known_issues: the 'five Amel 50s with damaged forestays' count relies on a paywalled owners' forum and cannot be independently verified.
stability.ballast_type: a single precise '26 percent' ballast ratio is unreliable because displacement figures differ across sources; treated as approximate.
verify.verdict: Reliable and well-sourced. The Amel 60 is a 2018-onward GRP centre-cockpit sloop by Berret-Racoupeau, European Yacht of the Year 2020, and the modern single-masted successor to the ketch-rigged Amel 55. Confirmed across multiple credible sources: CE Category A; the deliberate ketch-to-sloop switch and its rationale; the keel-stepped carbon Lorima furling mast; all-electric furling for main, genoa and staysail run from a deep, hardtop-sheltered central cockpit; six watertight compartments; a Furuno autopilot, 17.5 kVA generator, 720Ah lithium bank and ~530W solar as standard; 900L fuel and ~700L water; a sail-area/displacement ratio of 19.7 with ~7 knots in 8-10 knots true and 200-plus-mile days; option prices for heating (~€18,000), watermaker (~€18,500), air conditioning (~€26,000) and the cutter rig (~€20,000); maintenance-free teak-effect decks; three Amel service bases; and a launch base price near €1.65M ex VAT rising to ~€2.5M inc VAT by 2024. The main honest gaps are the unpublished AVS and STIX numbers and the absence of any documented storm-survival report; known cautions are handling/ergonomic (steering feel, no propwalk, halyard-wrap risk, sharp grab handles) rather than structural. Confidence raised from 0.71 to 0.83 after deeper sourcing corrected the mast-type error, confirmed the generator and lithium standard fit, established the SA/D figure, and added pricing and deck-construction facts.

cold_score.rationale: The Amel 60 scores well for cold and high-latitude shelter despite being aimed at warm-water cruising. Its strongest cold-weather asset is the deep central cockpit fully enclosed by a hardtop, wrap-around windshield and sliding sunroof, with all sail furling controllable from inside the enclosure, so the crew rarely has to leave shelter. The keel-stepped carbon mast, resin-infused foam-cored topsides over a solid GRP bottom, and high-density insulation throughout add to a warm, quiet interior, and the six watertight compartments add reassurance in any climate. Diesel hydronic heating is available but only as a roughly €18,000 option rather than standard, and the boat's cold-water tankage and heated-tank details are not published, which is the main thing keeping this short of a top mark. Overall this is a genuinely sheltered, well-built hull that would handle cool and high-latitude passages comfortably once heating is fitted.
cold_score.basis: Fully enclosed hardtop central cockpit with inside-furling control; keel-stepped carbon mast; foam-cored insulated topsides; six watertight compartments; heating optional not standard.

tropic_score.rationale: The Amel 60 is fundamentally a tropical world cruiser and scores strongly here. The fixed hardtop gives genuine permanent shade with standing headroom rather than a fabric bimini, and opening roof sections, multiple hatches and portholes keep the protected cockpit and saloon ventilated; air conditioning is available as an option for full climate control. A 180-litre refrigerator and an induction galley run off the 720Ah lithium bank handle provisioning, and the optional watermaker makes the boat largely water-independent. Its sail-area-to-displacement ratio of 19.7 sits in the reasonable-performance band, so it sails respectably in the moderate trade-wind breezes typical of tropical passages, reaching about 7 knots in 8-10 knots of wind and posting 200-plus-mile days. The points it loses are that air conditioning, watermaker and extra freezing are paid options rather than standard, and that in genuinely light airs the roughly 26-28 tonne displacement needs real wind to move. This corrects an earlier assessment that treated the boat as a poor light-air performer; the published 19.7 ratio shows it is a moderate, not weak, performer.
tropic_score.basis: Permanent hardtop shade and opening roof; strong ventilation; 180L fridge and induction galley on 720Ah lithium; optional watermaker and AC; SA/D 19.7 gives reasonable trade-wind performance.

stability_score.rationale: No published Angle of Vanishing Stability or STIX figure exists for the Amel 60 in any accessible source, so the stability rating rests on its CE certification and design. The boat is confirmed CE Category A (Ocean), the highest recreational design category, which on its own maps to a strong stability score. Supporting design factors point the same way: a roughly 7.2-tonne ballast keel for a ballast ratio near 26 percent, a deep single keel, and six watertight compartments that limit downflooding if the hull is breached. Confidence is held a little below the CE figure alone because the AVS and STIX numbers are genuinely absent and the ballast ratio is approximate, so the Category A certification is the primary stability evidence rather than a measured righting-moment curve.
stability_score.basis: CE Category A (Ocean) confirmed; ~7.2t ballast keel, ratio near 26 percent; six watertight compartments. AVS and STIX not published.

storm_verdict.text: There is no published magazine storm test or documented owner survival-conditions report for the Amel 60, so its heavy-weather verdict rests on design and one delivery passage rather than firsthand storm accounts. The boat is CE Category A certified for ocean use, and its heavy-weather design is genuinely strong: the deep central cockpit sits near the hull's pivot point where motion is least and is fully enclosed by a hardtop and windshield, the electric furling for main, genoa and optional cutter staysail can all be worked from inside that shelter with manual backups, the deck is ringed by a solid high guardrail, and the hull is divided into six watertight compartments. The clearest weaknesses for storm conditions are the light, indirect steering feel from the long cable runs to the twin rudders, which could reduce helm feedback as speed builds, and the documented furling-genoa halyard-wrap risk, which loads the forestay exactly when reliable sail handling matters most. One owner couple sailed the boat from France to the Canaries for an ARC and, after a heavy early learning curve on the boat's systems, reported full confidence in it by the time they reached Las Palmas. Beyond that, there is not enough firsthand data to characterise how the boat behaves in survival conditions.

refit_projection.assumptions: These are near-new boats: first hulls date to 2020, so as of 2026 the fleet is roughly six years old. That flips the usual refit picture: there is no age-driven engine repower, no seacock renewal, no teak-deck restoration (the decks are synthetic teak-effect), and no standing-rigging replacement due yet. Instead the spend is commissioning and offshore-readiness optioning. The low end assumes a well-specified boat needing only inspection, electronics tidy-up and safety gear; the high end assumes a base boat that still needs the major paid options (watermaker, air conditioning, extra sails, stern thruster) added for serious world cruising. Watermaker, air conditioning and heating are listed as conditional add-only items because they are factory options that many but not all boats already carry. EUR option prices from the magazine tests (heating ~€18,000, watermaker ~€18,500, AC ~€26,000, cutter rig ~€20,000) are shown with indicative USD bands; exact dollar cost moves with the exchange rate. All figures are estimates based on these option prices and standard service intervals, not Amel-60-specific refit invoices.

by_item.system: Standing rigging inspection (full re-rig not yet due)
by_item.note: Hulls from 2020 are years short of the 10-15 year wire-rigging interval. Budget for professional inspection and terminal checks only; a full re-rig would be a later-life cost.
Furuno autopilot is standard and recent. Budget for AIS, a second nav display or tablet, Starlink/Iridium comms, and redundant handheld GPS/VHF rather than a full electronics replacement.
Standard ~530W hardtop solar and a 720Ah lithium bank are capable but heavily loaded by the induction galley and AC. Adding solar or future-proofing the bank cuts generator hours.

by_item.system: Watermaker — CONDITIONAL, add only if not fitted
by_item.note: Factory option about €18,500. Excluded from the base totals; add it if the specific boat lacks a watermaker, since it is the key system for water-independent passage making.

by_item.system: Air conditioning — CONDITIONAL, add only if not fitted
by_item.note: Factory option about €26,000. Excluded from base totals; add for tropical liveaboard comfort if the boat was not optioned with it.

Electric furling Code 0 (~€18,000), gennaker (~€12,500) and self-tacking cutter staysail (~€20,000) are the main passage-making sail additions. At six years the working sails are mid-life, not due for replacement.

by_item.system: Stern thruster — CONDITIONAL, for tight-quarters handling
by_item.note: Twin rudders give no propwalk; a stern thruster greatly eases docking a 60-foot, ~27-tonne boat with a couple aboard. Add only if not already fitted.
Offshore liferaft, EPIRB, jacklines and harnesses, storm sails, plus heavier anchor/chain and a windlass service for world cruising.
The hardtop is structural, so canvas is limited to the optional cockpit enclosure and sail covers; tropical UV accelerates wear, so budget a soft-goods refresh.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furling-genoa halyard pre-rigged, plus a halyard organiser or anti-wrap collar
provisioning_specific.why: Halyard wrap around the furling swivel is the best-documented critical failure mode for this design and its Amel 50 predecessor. A pre-rigged spare and disciplined halyard tension prevent a wrap that can load and damage the forestay on passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Emergency tiller plus steering-cable and quadrant spares for the twin-rudder system
provisioning_specific.why: The magazine tests single out the long steering cable runs to the twin rudders and the light, indirect feel. Cable runs are a wear point, so a confirmed emergency tiller and spare cable parts are prudent offshore insurance.

provisioning_specific.item: 17.5 kVA generator service kit (filters, impellers, oil, belts)
provisioning_specific.why: The standard 17.5 kVA generator is run regularly to feed the lithium bank, induction galley, watermaker and air conditioning. A full service kit keeps the boat's electrical heart alive between the three Amel service bases.

provisioning_specific.item: Lithium BMS fuses, shunts and a cell-monitoring tool
provisioning_specific.why: The 720Ah lithium bank powers cooking, refrigeration and house loads. Protection spares and a monitoring tool let you catch a cell-level fault before it takes down the whole bank, since these proprietary parts are hard to source underway.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filters and membrane pickling solution (if watermaker fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: The optional watermaker is the key to water-independent passages. Tropical waters foul membranes fast, so pre-filters and pickling chemicals protect it across long gaps between use.

provisioning_specific.item: Lorima carbon furling-mast service spares and inspection routine
provisioning_specific.why: The keel-stepped Lorima carbon furling mast is proprietary and central to the boat. Furling-mast bearing service parts and a regular delamination/inspection routine protect the single biggest rig component.

provisioning_specific.item: Watertight-door and hatch gasket spares for the six compartments
provisioning_specific.why: The six-compartment subdivision only protects the boat if its seals are intact. Gaskets degrade over years, so spares preserve the watertight boundaries that make the boat hard to sink.

provisioning_specific.item: Provisioning plan for a couple: 700L water plus watermaker, 180L fridge, induction galley
provisioning_specific.why: With 700L freshwater, a large fridge and an induction galley fed by lithium, a couple can stock for multi-week passages. With the optional watermaker, water is effectively unlimited, so freezer space and dry-store volume become the real planning constraint rather than tankage.

provisioning_specific.item: Padded or sleeved covers for the guest-cockpit grab handles
provisioning_specific.why: The guest-cockpit grab handles are described as angular and sharp. Chafe-guard sleeves cut the risk of hand injury when crew grip them under load in a seaway, at minimal cost.

provisioning_specific.item: Stern thruster (if not fitted) for marina and anchorage handling
provisioning_specific.why: Twin rudders give no propwalk, making the boat awkward in tight quarters under power. A stern thruster materially improves docking and anchorage safety on a 60-foot, ~27-tonne boat handled by two.

field_reports.title: Stiff and seaworthy in 25 knots and 2m seas
field_reports.account: In YACHT magazine's test off Port Ginesta, the Amel 60 proved 'stiff enough' in 25-knot winds with waves over 2 metres despite a relatively modest ballast ratio. In moderate conditions (15–18 knots, ~1.5m waves) it held composed speed, and the testers noted around 7 knots under furling main and Code Zero in 8–10 knots, touching near its 10-knot hull speed with a gennaker in a fresh midday breeze.
field_reports.conditions: Up to 25 knots, 2m+ seas; also 8–10 knots making ~7 knots

source.title: Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect — YACHT

field_reports.title: Light-air test leaves heavy weather unproven
field_reports.account: Yachting World's Matthew Sheahan tested the Amel 60 in only 6–7 knots and flat water, making 5.4 knots with the 26-tonne boat — explicitly noting he 'didn't get to sail in a stronger breeze and bigger seas.' He praised the electrically furled Code 0 as 'a doddle,' found single-handing easy thanks to the well-sorted control panel, but flagged long steering-cable runs to the twin rudders and suspected finger-light helm feel would not be the boat's strong card.
field_reports.conditions: 6–7 knots true, flat water, 5.4 knots boatspeed

source.title: Amel 60 review: a true benchmark for quality — Yachting World

field_reports.title: Decoupled helm, but rock-solid autopilot
field_reports.account: YACHT's testers reported a 'decoupled' steering feel with reduced feedback on the Amel 60, attributing it to twin rudders set far aft whose pre-balance they judged too strong and transmission too indirect, which limits agility versus rivals. Upwind height was an average ~50 degrees. They emphasized this matters little in practice on a boat where the Furuno autopilot — which performed 'extremely precisely and reliably' — does the steering almost all the time.

source.title: Test Amel 60: Comfortable luxury yacht with a wow effect — YACHT

field_reports.title: Entropie crosses the Atlantic in 11d 19h
field_reports.account: Near-novice owners Jeremie Dubois-Lacoste and Deanna sailed their new Amel 60 Entropie across the Atlantic with the ARC, after coastal-hopping down France and Portugal to build confidence. They crossed in 11 days 19 hours — light, unstable winds early, then 15–17-knot trades with squalls in the last 48 hours — running downwind under poled-out genoa and main at night. Jeremie, with only about 18 months of sailing experience, called the crossing 'much less daunting than I was expecting.'
field_reports.conditions: Light/unstable early; 15–17 knot trades with squalls late

source.title: Advice on making an Atlantic crossing from people who have done it — Yachting World

field_reports.title: Near-100% cockpit-only sailing
field_reports.account: Reviewing the Amel 60 at the 2024 Cannes show, Lars Reisberg of no-frills-sailing described a near-100% 'cockpit-only' concept: all furling, winches and running rigging operate by push-button from the helm without leaving the protected center cockpit, which he noted gives the least motion in harsh conditions. He highlighted dual windlasses with separate anchor chains for world-cruising redundancy and a substantial bow locker doubling as a crash box, while conceding that '99 per cent of the time steering is done by the autopilot anyway.'

field_reports.title: Twin rudders aid Med stern-to and redundancy
field_reports.account: boats.com's overview of the Berret-Racoupeau-designed Amel 60 noted that its twin rudders reduce drag when heeled, give a more surefooted feel underway, and provide redundancy if one is damaged — with the shallower blades also easing stern-to mooring for owners who spend time in the Mediterranean. The reviewer called the boat reassuringly easy to handle shorthanded, with the deckhouse/dodger grown to winter-garden size for crew protection.

source.title: Four of the best Amel cruising yachts — boats.com
